answer:
\boxed{A}

The first passage frames the Cawnpore massacres as a shocking inversion of racial hierarchy, where "black men" (the colonized) dared to kill their "masters" (the British), emphasizing the perceived betrayal of a "superior" race by an "inferior" one. It highlights the mutineers' defiance of British dominance and the racial/religious tensions that fueled the revolt. The tone underscores the British perspective of outrage at this reversal of power.
